title: The Complexity of Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonians
abstract: Despite having an unnatural definition, $\mathsf{StoqMA}$ plays a central role in Hamiltonian complexity, e.g., in the classification theorem of the complexity of Hamiltonians by Cubitt and Montanaro (SICOMP 2016). Moreover, it lies between the two randomized extensions of $\mathsf{NP}$, $\mathsf{MA}$ and $\mathsf{AM}$. Therefore, understanding the exact power of $\mathsf{StoqMA}$ (and hopefully collapsing it with more natural complexity classes) is of great interest for different reasons. In this work, we take a step further in understanding this complexity class by showing that the Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonians problem ($\mathsf{StoqSH}$) is in $\mathsf{StoqMA}$. Since Stoquastic Local Hamiltonians are $\mathsf{StoqMA}$-hard, this implies that $\mathsf{StoqSH}$ is $\mathsf{StoqMA}$-complete. We complement this result by showing that the separable version of $\mathsf{StoqSH}$ is $\mathsf{StoqMA}(2)$-complete, where $\mathsf{StoqMA}(2)$ is the version of $\mathsf{StoqMA}$ that receives two unentangled proofs.
summary: This paper establishes that the problem of finding the ground state energy of a sparse stoquastic Hamiltonian is complete for the complexity class StoqMA. It also demonstrates that the power of a stoquastic verifier does not increase with the number of unentangled provers beyond two, providing a significant result for quantum complexity theory.
main_problem: The paper aims to determine the computational complexity of the Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonian problem (StoqSH) and investigate the power of the complexity class StoqMA when multiple unentangled proofs are provided.
main_result: The authors prove that StoqSH is StoqMA-complete and that the separable version of this problem is StoqMA(2)-complete, effectively showing that k unentangled Merlins can be simulated by two.
method: The work employs a Kitaev-style circuit-to-Hamiltonian construction, a sparse decomposition of Hamiltonians into 1-sparse terms, and a product test using SWAP tests to simulate multiple unentangled provers.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonians, which are matrices where off-diagonal elements are non-positive, ensuring the ground state has non-negative amplitudes, and where each row has at most polynomial non-zero entries.
key_observables: The ground state energy (specifically the minimum eigenvalue) and the acceptance probability of the quantum verifier.
important_parameters: The energy gap (alpha - beta) which is assumed to be at least 1/poly(n), the sparsity d, and the completeness and soundness parameters (a, b, c, s).
main_assumptions: The proof assumes the existence of oracles to access sparse Hamiltonian entries and relies on the ability to decompose the Hamiltonian into 1-sparse terms.
figures_summary: Figure 3 shows a plot in the completeness vs. soundness plane illustrating the region where the simulation of k Merlins by two Merlins holds.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the complexity classes and the StoqSH problem, then proceeds to prove the StoqMA-completeness of StoqSH, followed by an investigation into the separable version and the simulation of multiple unentangled provers (StoqMA(k) to StoqMA(2)).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2: Complexity classification of Hamiltonian problems with separable witness under locality, sparsity, and stoquasticity constraints.
